author: Novatian
translation: Robert Ernest Wallis (ANF vol. 5), public domain
note: Chapter title: 'And Indeed that Christ Was Not Only Man, But God Also; That Even as He Was the Son of Man, So Also He Was the Son of God.' A mid-third-century argument that Scripture proclaims Christ BOTH as man and as God, and that taking only one half wrecks the faith — 'being of both, He is both.' DELICACY: the wider chapter runs a long two-natures antithesis in which each 'as man' limb is deliberately paired with an 'as God' limb ('as man inferior to others, so as God greater than all'; 'as the Son in obedience to the Father'). Those 'as man' / 'obedience' clauses are predicated of Christ according to his HUMANITY and are meaningless torn from their paired clause — if this quote is ever extended, never carry one limb without the other. Novatian was later a schismatic over the lapsed; he is cited here solely for this christological argument, which the church retained. Verified verbatim (apologia-citations, web, 2026-07-20); translator Wallis confirmed, and the quote was extended to the end of its sentence so it is an unbroken run exactly as printed (an earlier draft stopped at the semicolon).
text: For Scripture as much announces Christ as also God, as it announces God Himself as man. It has as much described Jesus Christ to be man, as moreover it has also described Christ the Lord to be God. Because it does not set forth Him to be the Son of God only, but also the Son of man; nor does it only say, the Son of man, but it has also been accustomed to speak of Him as the Son of God. So that being of both, He is both, lest if He should be one only, He could not be the other. For as nature itself has prescribed that he must be believed to be a man who is of man, so the same nature prescribes also that He must be believed to be God who is of God; but if he should not also be God when he is of God, no more should he be man although he should be of man.

author: Novatian
translation: Robert Ernest Wallis (ANF vol. 5), public domain
note: Chapter argument: 'the authority of the faith enjoins, after the Father and the Son, to believe also on the Holy Spirit.' A mid-third-century witness that the Spirit is a PERSON who acts — placing prophets, instructing teachers, distributing gifts — not an impersonal force, and that the rule of faith is triadic. The ellipsis marks an omitted span within the same chapter. DELICACY (important, and the honest limit of this passage): Novatian's treatise never explicitly calls the Holy Spirit 'God', and his pre-Nicene pneumatology orders the Spirit after Father and Son; some read a subordinationist tendency. Cite this for the Spirit's PERSONAL agency and the triadic rule of faith — NOT as a proof-text for the Spirit's consubstantiality, which is secured later (Constantinople 381; see the 381 creed and the Cappadocian entries in this corpus). Verified verbatim against two independent PD hosts (apologia-citations, web, 2026-07-20); translator Wallis confirmed. TRANSCRIPTION WARNING: 'offers discrimination of spirits' and the ungrammatical 'and thus make the Lord's Church' are BOTH correct as ANF prints them — a widely-circulated web snippet misquotes the first as 'often', and 'make' must not be silently corrected to 'makes'; either change would break verbatim status.
text: Moreover, the order of reason, and the authority of the faith in the disposition of the words and in the Scriptures of the Lord, admonish us after these things to believe also on the Holy Spirit, once promised to the Church, and in the appointed occasions of times given. For He was promised by Joel the prophet, but given by Christ. … This is He who places prophets in the Church, instructs teachers, directs tongues, gives powers and healings, does wonderful works, offers discrimination of spirits, affords powers of government, suggests counsels, and orders and arranges whatever other gifts there are of charismata; and thus make the Lord's Church everywhere, and in all, perfected and completed.
